content.content: This is the 2018 AHA/ACC guideline for managing adults with congenital heart disease. It was developed by the American College of Cardiology and American Heart Association in collaboration with various societies related to thoracic surgery and cardiovascular care. The guideline includes contributions from multiple experts in the field.

content.Preamble: Since 1980, the American College of Cardiology (ACC) and American Heart Association (AHA) have developed clinical practice guidelines to improve cardiovascular health. These guidelines are based on systematic methods to evaluate evidence and are considered official policy.
content.Intended Use: Guidelines provide recommendations for patients with or at risk of cardiovascular disease. Although focused on the United States, they are created in collaboration with global organizations.
content.Clinical Implementation: Recommendations are most effective when adhered to by both practitioners and patients, emphasizing shared decision-making.
content.Methodology and Modernization: Continually revised by the ACC/AHA Task Force, guidelines are based on published standards and evolving technologies.

content.purpose: Introduction of an evolved format for presenting guideline recommendations called the 'modular knowledge chunk format'.
key_information: The guideline includes tables, synopses, and references for seamless updates with new evidence.
Ensures recommendations remain current with periodic comprehensive reviews and updates on a six-year cycle.
Focus on evidence-based methodologies and systematic review in developing recommendations.
Includes guideline-directed management and therapy for evaluation, testing, and treatment.
Ensures rigorous policies and procedures for avoiding undue influence in guideline development.
content.selection_of_members: Members from diverse backgrounds in terms of regions, sexes, and affiliations.
content.relationships: Guidelines ensure limited influence from industry or other entities.
content.class_of_recommendation_and_level_of_evi: Indicates the strength and benefit of a recommendation.

Comparative/Effectiveness Phrases: Treatment/strategy A is recommended/indicated in preference to treatment B
Treatment A should be chosen over treatment B
Treatment/strategy A is probably recommended/indicated in preference to treatment B
It is reasonable to choose treatment A over treatment B

Classes of Recommendation.Comments: Usefulness/effectiveness is unknown/unclear/uncertain or not well established
Associated with excess morbidity/mortality or should not be performed/administered/other

Levels of Evidence.Description: High-quality evidence from more than 1 RCT or meta-analyses of high-quality RCTs or corroborated by high-quality registry studies
Moderate-quality evidence from 1 or more RCTs or meta-analyses of moderate-quality RCTs
Moderate-quality evidence from 1 or more well-designed, well-executed nonrandomized studies, observational studies, or registry studies
Randomized or nonrandomized observational or registry studies with limitations or physiologic or mechanistic studies in humans
Consensus of expert opinion based on clinical experience

Notes: COR and LOE are determined independently.
A recommendation with LOE C does not imply weakness.
The method of assessing quality is evolving, including the application of standardized, widely used, and preferably validated evidence grading tools.

content.Title: 2018 ACHD Guideline
content.Purpose: A full revision of the 2008 ACC/AHA Guidelines for the Management of Adults With Congenital Heart Disease (CHD).

Review Process.Reviewed by: 3 official reviewers from ACC and AHA, and 1-2 reviewers from other societies

content.summary: This page contains overview text from the 2018 Adult Congenital Heart Disease (ACHD) guideline describing: increasing prevalence of ACHD due to improved pediatric cardiology and congenital cardiac surgery; improved survival into adulthood (survival to age 18 years now expected for 90% of children diagnosed with severe CHD); heterogeneity of ACHD population in anatomy and physiology; sequelae after childhood treatment; limitations of available data and reliance on expert consensus when data are lacking; recommendation that patients with ACHD be cared for in centers with ACHD expertise; and that the writing committee reviewed previous guidelines and statements (Table 2 referenced).
key_points: Prevalence of ACHD is rising because of success in pediatric cardiology and congenital cardiac surgery.
Survival to adulthood is high for many with severe congenital heart disease (example: survival to age 18 expected for ~90% of children diagnosed with severe CHD).
ACHD patients are heterogeneous in underlying anatomy, physiology, and prior repairs or palliations.
Most patients with ACHD have sequelae of native CHD or surgical repair that may manifest decades later.
Recommendations are based on available data; where data are lacking, first principles, extrapolation, and expert consensus guide recommendations.
Care in specialized ACHD centers is associated with better outcomes.

Background and Pathophysiology.Anatomic and Physiological Terms: The International Society for Nomenclature of Pediatric and Congenital Heart Disease mapped and archived nomenclature and developed standards for terminology. The International Paediatric and Congenital Cardiac Code (IPCCC) nomenclature is used.
Background and Pathophysiology.Severity of ACHD: Severity of disease in CHD is determined by anatomy, surgical repair, and current physiology. The ACHD Anatomic and Physiological (AP) classification system incorporates anatomical and physiological variables.
Background and Pathophysiology.The ACHD AP Classification: Captures the complexity of ACHD anatomy and physiology, accounting for correlated anatomical and clinical importance. Conditions like BAV, CoA, and TOF are mentioned, and severity is based on the highest relevant feature. The AP classification helps track clinical status and guide follow-up strategies.

content.Table: Physiological Variables as Used in ACHD AP Classification

Variables.Name: Aortopathy
Variables.Description: Aortic enlargement is common in some types of CHD and after some repairs. It may be progressive over a lifetime with no universally accepted threshold for repair.

Variables.Name: Arrhythmia
Variables.Description: Common in patients with ACHD; can cause deteriorating hemodynamics, valvular dysfunction, or ventricular dysfunction.

Variables.Name: Concomitant VHD
Variables.Description: Severity defined according to the 2014 VHD guideline.

Variables.Name: End-organ dysfunction
Variables.Description: Clinical and laboratory evidence of end-organ dysfunction including renal, hepatic, and pulmonary dysfunction.

Variables.Name: Exercise capacity
Variables.Description: Assessment of both subjective and objective exercise capacity is important.

Variables.Name: Hypoxemia/hypoxia/cyanosis
Variables.Description: Hypoxemia is defined as oxygen saturation measured by pulse oximetry at rest ≤90%.

Variables.Name: NYHA functional classification
Variables.Description: Based on functional capacity with four classes.

Variables.Name: Pulmonary Hypertension
Variables.Description: Broad term that encompasses pulmonary arterial hypertension, and hypertension with increased pulmonary vascular resistance.

Variables.Name: Shunt
Variables.Description: Intracardiac shunt is hemodynamically significant if there is channel enlargement or evidence of sustained Qp:Qs ≥1.5:1.

Variables.Name: Venous and arterial stenosis
Variables.Description: Includes aortic re-coarctation after CoA repair, branch PA stenosis, among others.

Simple: Repaired conditions without significant residual issues

content.KeyPersonnelAndServices: The guideline outlines the key personnel and services recommended for Adult Congenital Heart Disease (ACHD) programs, including ACHD board-eligible/certified cardiologists, congenital cardiac surgeons, nurses and nurse practitioners, and cardiac anesthesiologists with CHD expertise. Multidisciplinary teams should include specialties such as high-risk obstetrics, pulmonary hypertension, HF/transplant, genetics, hepatology, cardiac pathology, and more.
content.Services: Highlighted services include echocardiography, CHD diagnostic and interventional catheterization, and various specialized imaging and radiology techniques (CMR, CCT). There is an emphasis on information technology for data collection, database support, and quality assessment.
content.AccessToCare: The guideline recommends ensuring access to care for ACHD patients by facilitating smooth transitions from pediatric to adult care and promoting awareness for lifelong specialized care through educational programs. Challenges in access include inadequate resources, lack of comprehensive management, and different evaluation needs compared to adults with acquired cardiovascular disease.

Recommendations for Delivery of Care.Recommendation 1: Patients with ACHD AP classification IB-D, IIA-D, and IIIA-D should be managed in collaboration with an ACHD cardiologist.
Recommendations for Delivery of Care.Recommendation 2: Cardiac surgery and electrophysiological procedures for patients with ACHD should be performed by operators with expertise in CHD procedures, in collaboration with an ACHD cardiologist.

Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text: Patients with severe CHD in specialized centers have lower mortality than those managed without specialized care.
Management decisions for patients with ACHD must be based on sufficient data and guidelines.
ACHD clinical practices often involve multiple team members and specific expertise.
Training and certification pathways for ACHD expertise vary across medical and surgical specialties.
Specific ACHD training: Not generally available for cardiac anesthesiologists; many derive expertise through training in pediatric specializations.
Providers such as obstetricians, pulmonologists, and radiologists gain expertise through practice.
content.Importance of ACHD expertise: Patients with ACHD undergoing invasive procedures in specialized centers have better outcomes.

content.general_information: The image provides clinical guidelines on the delivery of care where Adult Congenital Heart Disease (ACHD) expertise may improve outcomes. It emphasizes the importance of specialist consultations, imaging, and interventions tailored to specific conditions of ACHD patients. Key areas include face-to-face consultations, collaborative care planning, imaging studies by experts, and electrophysiological procedures in specialized laboratories.

content.section: 3.4. Evaluation of Suspected and Known CHD
general_points: Evaluation tools helpful for adults with suspected or known cardiovascular disease are also valuable for patients with ACHD.
ECGs are used for various assessments including identification of sinus bradycardia or junctional rhythm, and evaluation of rhythm in patients with pacemakers.
CT, CMR, and cardiac catheterization may be more useful for ACHD patients compared to adults with acquired cardiovascular disease.
circumstances_for_CMR_CCT_TTE_use: Assessment of RV size and function in repaired TOF.
Identification of anomalous pulmonary venous connections.
Serial assessment of thoracic aortic aneurysms, especially when the dilation might extend beyond the echocardiographic windows.

Recommendations: A standard 12-lead electrocardiogram (ECG) is recommended in adults with CHD with serial assessment depending on the specific ACHD AP classification or when symptoms develop or worsen.

Ambulatory electrocardiographic monitoring should be performed in patients with CHD who are at risk of tachyarrhythmia, bradyarrhythmia or heart block, or when symptoms possibly of arrhythmic origin develop.

Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text: The ECG is an essential part of a complete cardiovascular evaluation of a patient with ACHD.
Asymptomatic arrhythmias seen in patients with ACHD may be associated with development of symptoms and increased risk of death.
Sinus node dysfunction is common in patients with atrial switch repairs of transposition of the great arteries (TGA).
Complete heart block is seen in patients with congenitally corrected transposition of the great arteries (CCTGA) or late after atrioventricular septal defect (AVSD) repair.

Recommendation for Ionizing Radiation Pr.Recommendation: Strategies to limit and monitor radiation exposure are recommended during imaging of patients with ACHD, with studies not involving ionizing radiation chosen whenever appropriate.

Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text: Low-dose ionizing radiation is a known carcinogen.
Certain levels of exposure similar to medical exposure have been associated with later malignancy.
Patients with ACHD have multiple potential exposures to low-dose ionizing radiation throughout their lifetimes.

Echocardiography.Recommendation 1: Intraoperative TEE is recommended to guide surgical repair of CHD in adults.
Echocardiography.Recommendation 2: Patients with ACHD should undergo transthoracic echocardiography (TTE) for initial assessment, with timing of serial assessment based on anatomic and physiological severity and clinical status.

CMR Imaging.Recommendation 1: In patients with ACHD who have or are at risk of developing RV enlargement and dysfunction, serial CMR is recommended for quantitative assessment of RV size and function.
CMR Imaging.Recommendation 2: CMR can be useful in the initial evaluation and serial assessment of selected patients with CHD based on anatomic complexity and clinical status.

Echocardiography: Intraoperative TEE has a substantial impact on patient care, leading to alteration of planned procedure in 14% of cases.
Echocardiography is indispensable for managing decisions and is used for initial and serial evaluations even without symptoms.
CMR Imaging: CMR provides data for assessment of RV size and function, advantageous over other imaging techniques.
CMR offers unrestricted access to heart and vessels noninvasively and without ionizing radiation.

content.overview: The guideline provides a comparison of imaging modalities for evaluating adults with congenital heart disease (ACHD), discussing factors such as radiation exposure, cost, and effectiveness in assessing ventricular volumes, valvular structure, coronary anatomy, and extracardiac vascular anatomy.

CCT Imaging.recommendation: CCT imaging is advised for ACHD when other diagnostic methods are insufficient to justify ionizing radiation exposure.
CCT Imaging.issues: Major disadvantage is the exposure to ionizing radiation, concerning for patients needing many assessments over their lifetime.
CCT Imaging.techniques: Gating CCT to ECG allows accurate imaging during cardiac cycles, reducing doses when assessing extracardiac structures.

Cardiac Catheterization.role: Standard in precise anatomical assessment or when additional data is needed, avoiding reliance on other diagnostic techniques.
recommendations: For ACHD patients at classification II and III, cardiac catheterization should be performed by experienced cardiologists.
Consider angiography for patients with a low CAD pretest probability to exclude significant CAD.

Recommendations.Description: In patients with ACHD, cardiopulmonary exercise testing (CPET) can be useful for baseline functional assessment and serial testing.
In symptomatic patients with ACHD, a 6-minute walk test can be useful to objectively assess symptom severity, functional capacity, and response to therapy.

Supportive Text: Patients with ACHD often overestimate their physical capabilities and underreport limitations.
CPET provides objective, reproducible, and repeatable assessment of physical function.
The 6-minute walk test has prognostic value even in severely impaired patients who cannot complete CPET.

Recommendations.Description: Clinicians caring for patients with CHD should deliver developmentally appropriate transition education to adolescent and young patients with CHD, and to their families/support network.

Supportive Text: Preparing a patient for independent cardiac care should start in early adolescence.
Transition education includes written and experiential efforts to educate about the specific heart disease, expectations, and concerns regarding CHD.
Lack of transition education can lead to gaps in care, increased hospitalizations, and morbidity.

recommendations.Recommendation: Clinicians should assess activity levels at regular intervals and counsel patients with ACHD about the types and intensity of exercise appropriate to their clinical status.
CPET can be useful to guide activity recommendations for patients with ACHD.
Cardiac rehabilitation can be useful to increase exercise capacity in patients with ACHD.

content.synopsis: Historically, guidelines for physical activity among patients with CHD have focused on restriction rather than promotion. Most patients with ACHD can safely engage in regular, moderate physical activity. However, certain conditions warrant more cautious recommendations. Knowledge of specific exercise capacity for patients with congenital heart defects is crucial, and self-directed activity is often 40% to 60% of maximal capacity. Regular exercise and rehabilitation can improve exercise capacity and reduce inactivity's negative effects.
recommendation_specific_supportive_text: Physical activity is beneficial to physical and mental health.
Conflicting evidence exists about physical activity levels in CHD patients.
Inactivity leads to reduced exercise performance; regular exercise improves capacity and symptoms.

recommendations.recommendation: Patients with ACHD should be evaluated for depression and anxiety.
Referral for mental health evaluation and treatment is reasonable in patients with ACHD.
Neurodevelopmental or neuropsychological testing may be considered in some patients with ACHD to guide therapies.

content.synopsis: Mental health and neurodevelopmental issues are common in patients with ACHD and may significantly affect QoL. Neurodevelopmental abnormalities are more frequent in children with complex disease. These disorders, such as impairment of cognition, social skills, and communication, are often underrecognized, though appropriate diagnosis and treatment may be beneficial. Anxiety and depression are prevalent, and psychological evaluation can identify up to 50% more patients with mood disorders. Neurodevelopmental impact includes cognitive, social skills, communication, and attention disorders. Genetic conditions such as 22q11 deletion and trisomy 21 are associated with these issues.

genes.context: This is particularly evident in patients with genetic conditions such as 22q11 deletion.

content.Title: 3.9. Concomitant Syndromes
content.Recommendation: 1. Genetic testing for 22q11 deletions is reasonable for patients with conotruncal cardiac defects.
content.Synopsis: Patients with genetic syndromes may exhibit phenotypic manifestations and associated CHD as features of a genetic abnormality. An underlying chromosomal abnormality exists in at least 10% of infants with CHD. Clinicians are advised to recognize the potential for undiagnosed genetic abnormalities affecting overall health and to pursue appropriate evaluation.

Common Genetic Syndromes with CHD.Clinical Features: Thymic and parathyroid hypoplasia, immunodeficiency, low-set ears, hypocalcemia, speech and learning disorders, renal anomalies, psychiatric disease
Common Genetic Syndromes with CHD.Common Cardiac Findings: IAA type B, aortic arch anomalies, truncus arteriosus, TOF

Common Genetic Syndromes with CHD.Clinical Features: Developmental disability, characteristic facial features, hypotonia, palmar crease
Tall stature, hypoplastic testes, delayed puberty, developmental disability
Facial anomalies, webbed neck, chest deformity, short stature, lymphatic abnormalities, bleeding abnormalities
Short stature, webbed neck, lymphedema, primary amenorrhea

Common Genetic Syndromes with CHD.Clinical Features: Elfin face, social personality, hearing loss, developmental delay, infantile hypercalcemia

Acquired Cardiovascular Disease.Patients with ACHD: Can acquire diseases like hypertension, coronary artery disease, vascular disease, stroke, and heart failure.
Acquired Cardiovascular Disease.Impact: The impact of acquired heart disease is increasing as the lifespan of patients with ACHD extends.
Acquired Cardiovascular Disease.Major Adverse Events: Include HF, coronary intervention, bypass surgery, malignant arrhythmia, and cardiac shock.
Acquired Cardiovascular Disease.Prevention and Treatment: Focus on risk factors such as diabetes, obesity, and hypertension.
Acquired Cardiovascular Disease.Healthy Lifestyle: Promoting healthy lifestyles and exercise is crucial.

Noncardiac Medical Issues.Hepatitis C: Patients with ACHD at risk for hepatitis C should be screened and vaccinated. Exposure during cardiac surgery is a concern.
Noncardiac Medical Issues.Consultation: With a hepatologist is recommended if liver disease is present.

Noncardiac Surgery.Optimization: Optimization and surveillance before and after surgery are essential for ACHD patients.
Noncardiac Surgery.ACHD AP Classification: Specific surgical procedures should be performed in expert settings (IB-D, IIA-D, IIIA-D* categories).

content.summary: Patients with ACHD are at higher operative risk than those without, as per the 2014 ACC/AHA guideline, which doesn't fully apply due to unique ACHD cardiac issues like ventricular dysfunction and arrhythmia mechanisms. Heightened surveillance and specialized evaluation are recommended for surgical management.

points: Clarify prior procedures and ACHD classification.
