content.Title: 3.13. Pregnancy, Reproduction, and Sexual Health

Recommendations.Recommendation: Women with congenital heart disease (CHD) should receive pre-pregnancy counseling with input from an ACHD cardiologist to determine maternal cardiac, obstetrical, and fetal risks, and potential long-term risks to the mother.

An individualized plan of care that addresses expectations and contingencies should be developed for and with women with CHD who are pregnant or who may become pregnant and shared with the patient and all caregivers.
Women with CHD receiving chronic anticoagulation should be counseled, ideally before conception, on the risks and benefits of specific anticoagulants during pregnancy.
Women with ACHD AP classification IIB-D, IIA-D, and IIIA-D* should be managed collaboratively during pregnancy by ACHD cardiologists, obstetricians, and anesthesiologists experienced in ACHD.

In collaboration with an ACHD cardiologist to ensure accurate assessment of pregnancy risk, patients at high risk of maternal morbidity or mortality should be counseled against becoming pregnant or be given the option of terminating pregnancy.
Men and women of childbearing age with CHD should be counseled on the risk of CHD recurrence in offspring.
Exercise testing can be useful for risk assessment in women with ACHD AP classification IC-D, IIA-D, and IIIA-D* who are considering pregnancy.
When either parent has CHD, it is reasonable to perform fetal echocardiography.

content.Synopsis: Most data regarding cardiac and obstetric risk to women with CHD during pregnancy derive from retrospective case series. Many women with CHD considering pregnancy may have received inconsistent guidance regarding pregnancy risks. Several risk scores have been developed to risk-stratify women with heart disease during pregnancy. All women with CHD should receive appropriate counseling regarding contraception choices. A multidisciplinary team that includes ACHD specialists and maternal-fetal medicine obstetricians is appropriate for achieving optimal outcomes.

Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text.text: Prepregnancy counseling for individualized risk assessment including maternal risks for pregnancy, delivery, postpartum, medications that may be teratogenic (e.g., ACE inhibitors/angiotensin receptor blockers), fetal CHD transmission discussion, and involvement of ACHD cardiologists to accurately assess pregnancy risks and delivery planning.

Care plan should address maternal cardiac risks based on individual anatomy and physiology with clear documentation, awareness of risks and expected outcomes, need for maternal cardiac monitoring, and contingency plans for CHD-related complications.

Chronic anticoagulation during pregnancy is associated with increased maternal bleeding/thrombotic events and fetal loss; warfarin has teratogenic risk. Choice of anticoagulant must balance maternal and fetal risks and be individualized; mechanical valve patients treated per GDMT.

Hemodynamic changes of pregnancy, labor, and delivery can cause decompensation in some women with CHD; management should involve ACHD, maternal-fetal medicine, and anesthesiology expertise to anticipate and mitigate maternal/fetal adverse outcomes.

Women at high risk include those meeting WHO maternal cardiac risk classification IV (examples: PAH of any cause; severe systemic ventricular dysfunction with LVEF <30% and/or NYHA III-IV symptoms; severe left heart obstruction; severe native coarctation). These patients have extremely high maternal mortality/severe morbidity and pregnancy termination should be discussed.
Prepregnancy counseling about CHD recurrence risk in offspring aids family planning and delivery decisions and allows time to answer parents' questions.

Cardiopulmonary exercise testing (CPET) before conception can predict maternal and neonatal outcomes; a blunted heart rate response to exercise in women with CHD is linked to higher risk of adverse maternal and neonatal events.

If the patient or partner is pregnant, there is increased risk of CHD in the offspring; fetal echocardiography may help define presence of CHD and determine course of action at delivery. Prenatal diagnosis may improve neonatal survival though study biases limit definitive benefit estimates.

Contraception Recommendations.Recommendation: Women of childbearing potential with CHD should be counseled about risks associated with pregnancy and appropriate contraceptive options (S3.13.2-1–S3.13.2-3).

Estrogen-containing contraceptives are potentially harmful for women with CHD who are at high risk of thromboembolic events (e.g., cyanosis, Fontan physiology, mechanical valves, prior thrombotic events, PAH) (S3.13.2-4, S3.13.2-5).

content.Synopsis: The guideline discusses contraceptive use in women with congenital heart disease (CHD) and the importance of balancing the risks of pregnancy. It highlights the lack of data on various contraceptive techniques in patients with adult congenital heart disease (ACHD).
Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text: Contraceptive choices must be tailored to individual risks and benefits for each patient.
Recommended contraceptive methods include combined hormonal contraception, progestin-only methods, intrauterine devices, barrier methods, and permanent sterilization.
Consideration is needed for women at high risk of thrombosis, especially those with cyanosis, Fontan physiology, prior thrombosis, or pulmonary arterial hypertension (PAH).
Menstrual cycle disorders, infertility, sexual function, and heart failure are noted concerns in women with CHD.
content.Infertility Treatment: Menstrual cycle disorders are prevalent in women with CHD. Infertility treatment requires careful consideration and potentially involves referral to a reproductive endocrinologist. No specific guidelines are available for women with different types of CHD in this context.
content.Sexual Function: Sexual health is an important aspect of quality of life. There is evidence of sexual health concerns among adults with CHD, with erectile dysfunction noted in a significant percentage of adult men with CHD. Providers are encouraged to discuss these issues openly with patients.
content.Heart Failure: Consultation with ACHD and heart failure specialists is recommended for patients with ACHD and heart failure or severe ventricular dysfunction.

content.Synopsis: HF (Heart Failure) is significant in ACHD (Adults with Congenital Heart Disease) and is associated with increased morbidity and mortality. Currently, GDMT (Guideline-Directed Medical Therapy) is preferable to no treatment, despite the lack of data supporting specific treatment recommendations for patients with ACHD. Clinical management expectations should be tempered, especially for those with acquired CVD as CHD patients were not included in guideline development.
content.HF Management: HF is common in ACHD and can result from valve dysfunction, shunts, arrhythmias, and ventricular dysfunction requiring evaluation and treatment. Advanced therapies may be challenging and should be timely.
content.Heart Transplant: Due to HF prevalence, heart transplantation is essential but complicated by limited data and donor availability. Current risk markers place ACHD patients at a disadvantage, often requiring 'exception' status.
content.Multiorgan Transplant: Although rare, considered for patients with CHD experiencing irreversible pulmonary hypertension. Multiorgan transplantation should involve a multidisciplinary approach.

content.Clinical Guidelines: The document discusses the use of palliative care for patients with Adult Congenital Heart Disease (ACHD), emphasizing individualized end-of-life care discussions. It highlights the complexity of transplant outcomes in patients with liver dysfunction and the need for multidisciplinary data gathering. Complications such as secondary erythrocytosis and iron deficiency are common in cyanotic individuals, and specific treatments are suggested for these conditions.

Recommendations.Recommendation: Discussion of end-of-life issues and advance directives can be beneficial for patients with ACHD or their surrogates.

Key Points: Predicting prognosis in ACHD is difficult.
There is a gap between patient-reported interest in end-of-life discussions and those acknowledged by providers.
Secondary erythrocytosis and iron deficiency are common in cyanotic heart disease and need specific treatment.

content.overview: The document discusses cyanotic heart disease (ACHD) and associated management strategies. It highlights the complexity of hyperviscosity due to altered blood properties and suggests managing symptoms through fluid therapy and evaluation for iron deficiency. The guideline lists potential manifestations such as myocardial, cerebral, and retinal blood flow alterations along with renal complications. Pharmacological treatment explores the use of therapies common in heart failure and arrhythmia, with emphasis on beta blockers, though with caution due to varied efficacy in patients with systemic right ventricles and pacing devices.
management_recommendations: Hydration and symptomatic treatment are recommended over phlebotomy unless necessary.
Supplementary oxygen may not be beneficial unless hypoxemia is demonstrated.
Avoidance of preventative measures that increase hypoxia risks unless critically warranted.
Non-estrogen contraception is recommended; intrauterine devices are preferred.
Travel precautions for oxygen management include supplemental oxygen and anticoagulation adjustment for elevated hematocrit.

diagnostic_recommendations: Pulse oximetry at rest and during exercise is recommended for evaluation of adults with unoperated or repaired ASD with residual shunt to determine the direction and magnitude of the shunt.
CMR, CCT, and/or TEE are useful to evaluate pulmonary venous connections in adults with ASD.
Echocardiographic imaging is recommended to guide percutaneous ASD closure.
therapeutic_recommendations: In adults with isolated secundum ASD causing impaired functional capacity, right atrial and/or RV enlargement, surgical closure to reduce RV volume and improve exercise tolerance is recommended, provided certain pressure conditions are met.
Adults with primum ASD or sinus venosus defect causing functional impairment and enlargement of heart sections should have surgical repair unless contraindicated.

In asymptomatic adults with secundum ASD and heart enlargement, surgical or transcatheter closure is recommended to reduce RV volume and improve functional capacity when specific vascular conditions are met.
Surgical closure of a secundum ASD in adults is reasonable when performed alongside another surgical procedure if a large enough left-to-right shunt is present.
pharmacological_therapies: Endothelin-receptor antagonists and phosphodiesterase type-5 inhibitors benefit Eisenmenger syndrome patients.

content.Synopsis: ASDs (Atrial Septal Defects) are common and may result from various anatomic defects such as secundum ASD, primum ASD, and sinus venosus defect. These defects can lead to right heart enlargement and RV dysfunction. Some patients may experience right-to-left shunting or paradoxical embolism, and arrhythmias. Percutaneous or surgical closure is the main therapy for those with hemodynamic or clinical consequences. Severe PAH is a contraindication to closure.
Guideline Recommendations: Percutaneous or surgical closure may be considered for adults with ASD when left-to-right shunt (Qp:Qs) is 1.5:1 or greater.

ASD closure should not be performed in adults with PA systolic pressure greater than two-thirds systemic, pulmonary vascular resistance greater than two-thirds systemic, and/or a net right-to-left shunt.
Pulse oximetry is useful for defining shunt direction at rest and with exercise.
TTE (Transthoracic Echocardiography) has limited use in assessing anomalous pulmonary venous connections in adults with ASD.
Use of TEE (Transesophageal Echocardiography) and intracardiac echocardiography is recommended for imaging to aid closure of ASD.
Recommendations are focused not on ASD associated with complex CHD, but isolated ASDs.
Other Recommendations: Table 13 should be referred to for routine follow-up and testing intervals.
The use of additional imaging modalities such as CMR or CT for delineating pulmonary venous connections.

Criteria.Pulmonary Vascular Resistance: > 1/3 systemic vascular resistance OR PASP > 50% systemic

content.Title: Anomalous Pulmonary Venous Connections
content.Description: Clinical guidelines for the evaluation and treatment of anomalous pulmonary venous connections in adults.

Recommendations.Recommendation: CMR or CTA is recommended for evaluation of partial anomalous pulmonary venous connection.
Cardiac catheterization can be useful in adults with partial anomalous pulmonary venous connection to further define hemodynamics.
Surgical repair is recommended for patients with partial anomalous pulmonary venous connection when functional capacity is impaired.
Repair of partial anomalous pulmonary venous connection is recommended at the time of closure of a sinus venosus defect or ASD.
Repair of a scimitar vein is recommended in adults when functional capacity is impaired.
Surgery can be useful for right- or left-sided partial anomalous pulmonary venous connection in asymptomatic adults with RV volume overload.
Surgery can be useful for repair of a scimitar vein in adults with evidence of RV volume overload, with Qp:Qs 1.5:1 or greater.

content.Synopsis: Abnormal connection between a pulmonary vein and systemic vein results in right heart volume overload, similar to ASD. The most common connection involves the right upper pulmonary vein to the superior vena cava. The condition may lead to low-velocity venous flow impacts risk of thrombosis.

Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text: Cross-sectional imaging with CMR or CTA is ideal for delineating pulmonary venous connections, preferable due to non-use of ionizing radiation and ability to quantify shunting.
In higher-risk patients, invasive hemodynamic assessment is useful for pressure measurement, shunt magnitude, and responsiveness to pulmonary vasodilator therapy.
It is unusual for a single anomalous pulmonary venous connection of only 1 pulmonary lobe to justify surgical repair unless symptoms are present.
Surgical repair involving scimitar vein includes direct reimplantation into the left atrium, with potential challenges.
Pulmonary hypertension is associated with poor outcomes in these cases.
VSD closure is recommended based on specific hemodynamic criteria, including pulmonary vascular resistance and systemic pressure ratios.

Ventricular septal defects (VSDs).Initial presentation: VSDs create a volume load to the left heart.
Ventricular septal defects (VSDs).Commonality: Most commonly encountered form of CHD.
Types: Isolated muscular and perimembranous VSDs are small and close spontaneously.
Large nonrestrictive defects in cyanotic patients with Eisenmenger syndrome.

Management.Watchful waiting: Small restrictive defects of the muscular or membranous septum may be watched.

Associated syndromes: Eisenmenger syndrome: Cyanosis/hypoxemia due to right-to-left shunt reversal.
Surgical considerations: Early VSD closure improves survival rates.
Aortic valve surgery may be needed for AR.
Risk factors: Untreated VSD leads to increased risk of IE, involving tricuspid and pulmonic valves.
Additional considerations: Patients with PAH may need special care to reduce mortality risks.

progression.AR: Progressive AR due to perimembranous or supracristal VSD

right_to_left.confirmation: Confirm PAH diagnosis through invasive hemodynamic assessment (Class I)

content.notes: Combination therapy with bosentan and PDE-5 inhibitor if symptomatic improvement does not occur with either alone. ACHD indicates adult congenital heart disease.

content.Title: Atrioventricular Septal Defect

Recommendations.Recommendation: Cardiac catheterization can be useful in adults with atrioventricular septal defect when pulmonary hypertension is suspected.
Surgery for severe left atrioventricular valve regurgitation is recommended per GDMT indications for mitral regurgitation.

Surgery for primary repair of atrioventricular septal defect or closure of residual shunts in adults with repaired atrioventricular septal defect is recommended when there is a net left-to-right shunt (Qp:Qs ≥1.5:1), PA systolic pressure less than 50% systemic and pulmonary vascular resistance less than one third systemic.

Operation for discrete LVOT obstruction in adults with atrioventricular septal defect is reasonable with a maximum gradient of 50 mm Hg or greater, a lesser gradient if HF symptoms are present, or if concomitant moderate-to-severe mitral or AR are present.

Surgery for primary repair of atrioventricular septal defect or closure of residual shunts in adults with repaired atrioventricular septal defect may be considered in the presence of a net left-to-right shunt (Qp:Qs ≥1.5:1), if PA systolic pressure is 50% or more systemic, and/or pulmonary vascular resistance is greater than one third systemic.

Surgery for primary repair of atrioventricular septal defect or closure of residual shunts in adults with repaired atrioventricular septal defect should not be performed with PA systolic pressure greater than two thirds systemic, pulmonary vascular resistance greater than two thirds systemic, or a net right-to-left shunt.

content.Synopsis: AVSDs represent about 4% to 5% of congenital heart defects, including primum ASD, inlet VSD, and common atrioventricular valve. Occurrence in syndromes such as Down syndrome is noted. Surgical repair is common, and management requires monitoring for valve regurgitation and stenosis.

genes.context: There are no specific gene mentions in the provided text.

content.title: AVSD: Routine Follow-Up and Testing Intervals

notes: See Tables 3 and 4 for details on the ACHD AP classification system.
16-minute walk test or CPET, depending on the clinical indication.

atrioventricular_node.description: Displaced inferiorly in AVSD, associated with relative hypoplasia of the left anterior fascicle.
atrioventricular_node.implications: Regular monitoring for symptoms and screening with an ECG are important.

hemodynamic_assessment.role: Confirmatory tool for evaluation of pulmonary vasoactivity and prognostic significance for adults with shunts.

left_atrioventricular_valve.extrapolation: Criteria for surgery based on mitral valve.
left_atrioventricular_valve.risks: Potential differences from acquired mitral valve disease.

topics.title: Patent Ductus Arteriosus

diagnostic.recommendation: Measurement of oxygen saturation should be performed in feet and both hands in adults with a PDA to assess for the presence of right-to-left shunting.
In addition to the standard diagnostic tools, cardiac catheterization can be useful in patients with PDA and suspected pulmonary hypertension.

therapeutic.recommendation: PDA closure in adults is recommended if left atrial or LV enlargement is present and attributable to PDA with net left-to-right shunt, PA systolic pressure less than 50% systemic and pulmonary vascular resistance less than one third systemic.

PDA closure in adults may be considered in the presence of a net left-to-right shunt if PA systolic pressure is 50% or greater systemic, and/or pulmonary vascular resistance is greater than one third systemic.

PDA closure should not be performed in adults with a net right-to-left shunt and PA systolic pressure greater than two thirds systemic or pulmonary vascular resistance greater than two thirds systemic.

synopsis: The ductus arteriosus is a vascular connection between the aorta and PA present in fetal life. It typically closes shortly after birth but may remain patent in some individuals.
The incidence of PDA is around 0.3% to 0.8% of term infants, more common in females.
Many PDAs are closed in infancy or childhood with catheter-based or surgical approaches.

references: Table 16 for routine testing and follow-up intervals

recommendation_support_text: Cyanosis related to right-to-left shunting in PDA may require pulse oximetry in both feet and hands.
Invasive hemodynamic assessment is crucial for evaluating pulmonary vasoreactivity.
Closure may prevent development of PAH and other symptoms if signs of significant left-to-right shunt are present.

diagnostic: Adults with cor triatriatum sinister should be evaluated for other congenital abnormalities.
Evaluate for pulmonary vein stenosis in adults with prior repair and recurrent symptoms.
therapeutic: Surgical repair is recommended for obstruction or significant gradients across the membrane.

Synopsis.Cor triatriatum: Occurs when a membrane divides either the left atrium or right atrium. It is usually associated with other congenital malformations such as ASD and VSD.
Synopsis.Supravalvular mitral stenosis: Typically caused by a fibrous ring, separating the mitral valve from both the left atrial appendage and the pulmonary veins. It is often part of Shone complex.
Synopsis.Congenital Mitral Valve Disease: Anatomically complex and often accompanied by other lesions.

Cor Triatriatum Sinister.Surgery: Should be performed when there is a substantial gradient. Mean gradient in one series was at least 8 mm Hg.
Cor Triatriatum Sinister.Pulmonary Venous Stenosis: Does not usually progress over time and not associated with PAH.
Cor Triatriatum Sinister.Intervention in Low Gradients: May be warranted under specific circumstances.

Congenital Mitral Stenosis.Evaluation: Adults with congenital mitral stenosis should be evaluated for left-sided obstructive lesions.

content.Title: Recommendations for Subaortic Stenosis

Recommendations.Description: Stress testing for adults with LVOT obstruction to determine exercise capacity, symptoms, electrocardiographic changes, or arrhythmias may be reasonable in the presence of otherwise equivocal indications for intervention.
Surgical intervention is recommended for adults with subAS, a maximum gradient 50 mm Hg or more and symptoms attributable to the subAS.
Surgical intervention is recommended for adults with subAS and less than 50 mm Hg maximum gradient and HF or ischemic symptoms, and/or LV systolic dysfunction attributable to subAS.
To prevent the progression of AR, surgical intervention may be considered for asymptomatic adults with subAS and at least mild AR and a maximum gradient of 50 mm Hg or more.

Synopsis: SubAS may occur as a discrete membrane below the aortic valve in the LVOT.
SubAS tends to recur, particularly when initial resection is needed in childhood.
Surgical repair for subAS carries a 10% to 15% risk of complete heart block.
The guideline recommends intervention specifically for discrete membrane or tunnel-like obstruction.

Table.Title: Subaortic Stenosis: Routine Follow-Up And Testing Intervals

Recommendations.Exercise stress testing: Reasonable in assessment of exercise capacity, stress-induced arrhythmias, and ischemia in patients with subAS.
Recommendations.Surgical intervention: Considered in patients with severe subAS in the absence of obstructive coronary artery disease.
Recommendations.Progression of subAS: Tends to be progressive with age, patients with a resting maximum gradient >= 50 mm Hg may have progressive subAS.

Diagnostic Recommendations.Adults with bicuspid aortic valve: Should be evaluated for coarctation of the aorta by clinical examination and imaging studies.
Diagnostic Recommendations.First-degree relatives of patients: Reasonable to screen with echocardiography for valve disease and aortopathy.

content.Therapeutic Recommendation: In adults with bicuspid aortic valve stenosis and a noncalcified valve with no more than mild AR meeting indications for intervention per GDMT, it may be reasonable to treat with balloon valvuloplasty.
content.Indications for Aortic Valve Replacement: According to the 2014 VHD guideline, intervention may involve issues specific to congenital aortic valve disease, particularly in adults, with conditions influenced by factors such as lifestyle and anatomy.
content.BAV Prevalence and Genetic Consideration: BAV is the most prevalent congenital cardiac abnormality with a higher presence in males. It often occurs in an autosomal dominant pattern with variable penetrance.
content.CoA and BAV Association: CoA has a higher occurrence in males and is often associated with BAV, necessitating evaluation in such cases.
content.Echocardiogram Frequency: Routine follow-up and testing intervals for congenital aortic stenosis involve echocardiogram frequency depending on the stage.
content.Balloon Valvuloplasty Considerations: May improve symptoms in patients with noncalcified BAV stenosis, generally in young patients under 25 years of age. However, calcified BAV is associated with decreased efficacy and increased risk of AR, necessitating specialized collaboration for younger patients.

content.Title: Turner Syndrome Recommendations
content.Diagnostic Recommendation: Women with Turner syndrome should be evaluated for bicuspid aortic valve, coarctation of the aorta, and enlargement of the ascending aorta.
content.Therapeutic Recommendation: Prophylactic replacement of the aortic root or ascending aorta in adults with Turner syndrome is reasonable when the aortic diameter is 2.5 cm/m² or greater.
content.Synopsis: The management of valve dysfunction follows the 2014 VHD guideline. Aortopathy is common and involves the mid-ascending aorta. MRI and CCT measurement of aortic dimensions is not standardized. Pregnancy in Turner syndrome has an increased risk of aortic dissection, especially with existing aortic valve or aorta abnormalities.
Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text: Women with Turner syndrome are at risk of BAV, CoA, and aortic enlargement, which can lead to morbidity and mortality if untreated.
Early evaluation and accurate risk assessment are vital, considering factors such as exercise, pregnancy, or underlying aortic pathology.
Prophylactic surgery at lower diameters is reasonable in non-Turner aortopathy if rapid dilation is present.
content.Aortopathies: Several CHD subtypes/repairs are associated with aortic enlargement. BAV is the most common CHD with a strong aortopathy association, indicating increased risk for aortic complications. A dilated aortic root post-Ross procedure is uncommon, suggesting Ross procedures can be less aggressive than native BAV repairs.

Diagnostic.Recommendation: Aortic imaging using TTE, TEE, CMR, or CTA is recommended in adults with Williams syndrome or patients suspected of having supravalvular aortic stenosis.
Coronary imaging is recommended in patients with Williams syndrome and supravalvular aortic stenosis presenting with symptoms of coronary ischemia.

Therapeutic.Recommendation: Surgical repair is recommended for adults with supravalvular aortic stenosis with symptoms or decreased LV systolic function deemed secondary to aortic obstruction.
Coronary artery revascularization is recommended in symptomatic adults with supravalvular aortic stenosis and coronary ostial stenosis.

content.Synopsis: Supravalvular aortic stenosis is a condition often seen in patients with Williams syndrome or homozygous familial hypercholesterolemia. It includes stenosis near the sino-tubular junction and can lead to coronary ostial stenosis, risking SCD and anesthesia complications.

genes.context: Aortic imaging is recommended in adults with Williams syndrome, a condition associated with mutations in the ELN gene.

content.Clinical Focus: Management of supravalvular aortic stenosis and coarctation of the aorta.
Key Points: TEE helps assess coronary patency and detect flow turbulence.
Coronary angiography provides clarity of arterial anatomy.
Progression can lead to decreased LV systolic function and coronary revascularization may be needed.

Recommendations for Coarctation of the A.Recommendation: Initial and follow-up aortic imaging using CMR or CTA for adults with coarctation.
Measure resting blood pressure in upper and lower extremities in adults.
Ambulatory blood pressure monitoring for diagnosis and management.
Screening for intracranial aneurysms by MRA or CTA.

Therapeutic Recommendations.Recommendation: Surgical repair or catheter-based stenting is recommended for adults with hypertension and significant native or recurrent coarctation of the aorta.
GDMT is recommended for treatment of hypertension in patients with coarctation of the aorta.
Balloon angioplasty for adults with native and recurrent coarctation of the aorta may be considered if stent placement is not feasible and surgical intervention is not an option.

Synopsis.CoA: CoA typically occurs near the ductal remnant and left subclavian artery. Hypertension is the most common sequela, whether repaired or unrepaired.
Synopsis.BAV: BAV is commonly associated with CoA. Intracranial aneurysms may occur.
Synopsis.Aortic Aneurysms: Ascending aortic aneurysms are often found in those with BAV, with risk of aneurysms at coarctation repair sites in the descending thoracic aorta or arch.

Long-term Follow-up.Complications: Reoccurrence of aortic issues, aneurysms, pseudoaneurysms, and dissection are noted. Imaging recommended for long-term follow-up.
Long-term Follow-up.Unoperated Adults: Typically present with hypertension measured in the upper extremities.

Recommendations.Routine Testing: Table 21 suggests intervals based on physiological stage.

content.page_title: 2018 ACHD Guideline - Coarctation of the Aorta (CoA) and Valvular Pulmonary Stenosis (page e131)
content.source_citation: JACC Vol. 73, No. 12, April 2, 2019; Stout et al., 2018 ACHD Guideline (page e131)
coarctation_key_points: 3. Upper body systemic hypertension is prevalent in patients with unoperated coarctation and may be present in up to one third of patients after operative or transcatheter intervention. Ambulatory blood pressure monitoring can be useful to identify and manage ambulatory hypertension.

4. Multiple studies show increased frequency of intracranial aneurysm in adults with CoA. Approximately 10% of patients with CoA have intracranial aneurysms identified on MR angiography or CTA; increasing age is a risk factor. Screening/management uncertainties exist; intracranial aneurysms are less commonly found in children and teenagers with CoA.

5. Despite successful surgical or transcatheter repair, hypertension can persist and may not be identified at rest. Up to 80% of patients with prior CoA intervention manifest an abnormally elevated upper extremity exercise blood pressure response; peak blood pressure correlates with increased LV mass. Restenosis of repaired or stented regions may be identified by increased peak blood pressure response or exercise gradients.

6. Significant native or recurrent aortic coarctation is defined by several hemodynamic criteria (see definitions below). Anatomic evidence of CoA should be obtained by advanced imaging (CMR, CTA) before intervention. Best evidence to proceed with intervention includes systemic hypertension, an elevated upper/lower extremity blood pressure gradient, and echocardiography Doppler gradient plus anatomic confirmation.

7. Long-term complications of CoA are generally related to chronic upper body systemic hypertension; systemic hypertension should be identified by resting, ambulatory, or exercise blood pressure assessment and treated medically following guideline-directed medical therapy (GDMT).
8. Balloon angioplasty alone is associated with a higher rate of intimal tears and aneurysm formation compared with stent placement.

significant_coarctation_hemodynamic_thre: Upper extremity / lower extremity resting peak-to-peak gradient > 20 mm Hg
Mean Doppler systolic gradient > 20 mm Hg
Upper extremity / lower extremity gradient > 10 mm Hg or mean Doppler gradient > 10 mm Hg plus either decreased left ventricular (LV) systolic function or aortic regurgitation (AR)
Upper extremity / lower extremity gradient > 10 mm Hg or mean Doppler gradient > 10 mm Hg with collateral flow
exercise_findings_suggesting_restenosis_: Increased upper to lower extremity blood pressure gradient with exercise
Increased Doppler velocity across the coarctation site during exercise transthoracic echocardiography (TTE)
Increased peak blood pressure response correlated with increased LV mass

recommended_imaging: Echocardiography Doppler (resting and exercise when indicated)
Advanced anatomic imaging: Cardiovascular MR (CMR) or CT angiography (CTA)
imaging_and_evaluation.notes: Anatomic features (e.g., proximity to head/neck vessels, presence of concomitant aneurysm) influence whether surgery or stenting is optimal and whether a covered stent is needed.

intracranial_aneurysm_statements.prevalence: Approximately 10% of patients with CoA have intracranial aneurysms identified on MR angiography or CTA.
intracranial_aneurysm_statements.considerations: Increasing age identified as a risk factor; routine screening and management carry uncertainties; intracranial aneurysms less common in children/teenagers with CoA.

valvular_pulmonary_stenosis_section.heading: 4.3 Right-Sided Lesions — 4.3.1 Valvular Pulmonary Stenosis

recommendations_summary.recommendation: In adults with moderate or severe valvular pulmonary stenosis and otherwise unexplained symptoms of heart failure (HF), cyanosis from interatrial right-to-left communication, and/or exercise intolerance, balloon valvuloplasty is recommended (S4.3.1-1–S4.3.1-4).

In adults with moderate or severe valvular pulmonary stenosis and otherwise unexplained symptoms of HF, cyanosis, and/or exercise intolerance who are ineligible for or who failed balloon valvuloplasty, surgical repair is recommended (S4.3.1-1, S4.3.1-5–S4.3.1-8).
In asymptomatic adults with severe valvular pulmonary stenosis, intervention is reasonable.

valvular_pulmonary_stenosis_section.supplement_note: Referenced studies that support recommendations are summarized in the Online Data Supplement 39.

other_clinical_points: Systemic hypertension may not be consistently identifiable at rest; ambulatory blood pressure monitoring can aid in identification and management.
Multiple factors determine optimal management (surgery vs stenting): anatomy, presence of collateral flow, proximity to branch vessels, and concomitant aneurysm.

content.Synopsis: Valvular PS is a common congenital heart defect, occurring in up to 7% of children with CHD. It involves a dilated main PA and dysplastic valve cusps. Management depends on the degree of obstruction and RV pressure. Routine follow-up is recommended, and intervention is necessary in cases of severe stenosis or worsening symptoms. Pulmonary atresia with an intact ventricular septum is also discussed, highlighting variable surgical pathways.
content.Recommendations: 1. Intermediate or severe isolated pulmonary valve stenosis benefits from balloon valvuloplasty. 2. Surgical valvotomy is indicated when pulmonary annulus is not hypoplastic. 3. Significant relief can be achieved for severe stenosis, and interventions should be reconsidered if symptoms worsen.

content.Condition: Isolated PR after repair of PS

Footnotes.RV dilation: Significant PR causes RV dilation. If a patient has moderate or greater PR and normal RV size, further investigation may be required.
Footnotes.Symptoms: May include dyspnea, chest pain, and/or exercise intolerance referable to PR or otherwise unexplained.

Recommendations for Isolated PR After Re.Recommendation: In symptomatic patients with moderate or greater PR resulting from treated isolated pulmonary stenosis, with RV dilation or RV dysfunction, pulmonary valve replacement is recommended.
For asymptomatic patients with residual PR resulting from treatment of isolated pulmonary stenosis with a dilated right ventricle, serial follow-up is recommended.

In asymptomatic patients with moderate or greater PR resulting from treatment of isolated pulmonary stenosis with progressive RV dilation and/or RV dysfunction, pulmonary valve replacement may be reasonable.

Recommendations for Branch and Periphera.Recommendation: For adults with peripheral or branch PS, ongoing surveillance is recommended.
In adults with peripheral or branch PA stenosis, PA dilation and stenting can be useful.

Synopsis: Although many patients with valvular PS do not require intervention, some have PS that is severe enough to warrant intervention, often in infancy or childhood. PS can be alleviated by surgical valvotomy or with balloon valvuloplasty. Either surgical or catheter intervention may result in hemodynamically important PR that can result in symptoms, RV enlargement, and/or dysfunction requiring pulmonary valve replacement.
Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text: Patients with isolated PS who have previously undergone an intervention on the pulmonary valve require ongoing clinical follow-up and monitoring of PR, RV size and function, and functional capacity. This may include echocardiography, CPET, and advanced imaging.

Clinical Summary.pulmonary branch: Can be isolated or associated with syndromes like Noonan, Alagille, Williams, maternal rubella exposure.
Clinical Summary.imaging: TTE, CMR, CCT used for visualization and assessment.
Clinical Summary.interventions: Balloon angioplasty, stenting for branch PA stenosis, especially with TOF or Williams syndrome.

Surgical repair.Class I C-LD: Recommended for adults with unexplained HF symptoms.
Surgical repair.Class IIb C-LD: Considered for asymptomatic patients with severe gradient.

genes.context: Pulmonary branch and peripheral PS can occur in association with a syndrome (e.g., Noonan).
Pulmonary branch and peripheral PS can occur in association with a syndrome (e.g., Alagille).
Pulmonary branch and peripheral PS can occur in association with a syndrome (e.g., Williams).

content.Synopsis: Double-chambered right ventricle is uncommon in adults and involves hypertrophied muscle bundles in the RV cavity, leading to RVOT obstruction. It is commonly associated with a VSD. Alternative imaging or cardiac catheterization may be required for diagnosis and to understand the hemodynamic impact.

Recommendations.Text: Surgery typically involves the resection of obstructing muscle bundles and VSD closure if present.
Recommendations.Details: Patch enlargement of RVOT may be necessary, especially if exercise capacity decreases.

Diagnostic Recommendations.Recommendation: CMR is useful for determining anatomy, RV dimensions, and systolic function in adults with Ebstein anomaly.
TEE is useful for surgical planning if TTE images are inadequate.
Electrophysiological studies are useful in diagnostic evaluation.
Electrophysiological study is reasonable before surgical intervention on the tricuspid valve.

Therapeutic Recommendations.Recommendation: Surgical repair for adults with Ebstein anomaly and significant TR is recommended with specific symptoms or evidence of dysfunction.

content.Synopsis: Ebstein anomaly is an uncommon congenital heart defect occurring in about 0.005% of live births. It is a malformation of the tricuspid valve and the right ventricle that varies in severity. It can include asymptomatic adults or severe cases where infants do not survive. Ebstein anomaly may occur with other defects like ASD, VSD, and PS. Symptoms can include heart failure, cyanosis, and arrhythmias. Surveillance often includes echocardiography, CMR, rhythm assessment, pulse oximetry, and stress testing.

Recommendations.Catheter ablation: Recommended for adults with Ebstein anomaly and high-risk pathway conduction or multiple accessory pathways.
Recommendations.Surgical repair: Suggested for adults with significant TR if symptomatic conditions like RV enlargement or systemic desaturation are present.
Recommendations.Glenn anastomosis: May be considered if severe RV dilation or dysfunction is present but LV function and left atrial pressure aren't elevated.
Supportive Techniques: Echocardiography for anatomy and size assessment.
CMR for clinical care and decision-making support.
3D TEE for detailed valve anatomy and function.
Recommendations.Routine Testing: Involves follow-up by ACHD cardiologist, use of ECG, CMR, and tests like pulse oximetry.

content.Ebstein anomaly management: Approximately one third of adults with Ebstein anomaly and ventricular preexcitation have multiple accessory pathways, associated with a high risk of SCD and atrial tachyarrhythmia. Concealed accessory pathways may exist, and their identification is crucial before surgery. Surgical repair for Ebstein anomaly includes tricuspid valve repair, resection of accessory pathways, and atrial level shunt procedures. Surgery should be timed appropriately, considering functional ability and symptoms. Preoperative catheterization is important for assessing hemodynamics.
content.Tetralogy of Fallot (TOF) Recommendation: Recommendations include CMR for assessing ventricular and pulmonary function and anatomy. Coronary artery compression testing is suggested prior to interventions involving the pulmonary artery conduit.

content.title: Pulmonary Valve Replacement in Patients with TOF Repair and PR
content.purpose: Guideline for evaluation and treatment of pulmonary valve concerns post-TOF repair.

action.Yes: Evaluation by an ACHD cardiologist & advanced HF team (Class I)

steps.condition: Severely decreased LV or RV systolic function

Any 2 of the following: Mild or moderate RV or LV systolic dysfunction, Severe RV dilation (RVEDVi ≥160 mL/m², or RVESVi ≥80 mL/m², or RVEDVi ≥2x LVEDV), RVSP due to RVOT obstruction ≥2/3 systemic pressure, Progressive reduction in objective exercise tolerance

action.No: Check for residual lesions requiring surgical interventions

content.notes: Symptoms may include dyspnea, chest pain, and/or exercise intolerance referable to PR or otherwise unexplained. ACHD indicates adult congenital heart disease; HF, heart failure; LV, left ventricular; LVEDV, left ventricular end diastolic volume; PR, pulmonary regurgitation; RV, right ventricular; RVEDV, right ventricular end diastolic volume; RVEDVi, right ventricular end diastolic volume index; RVESVi, right ventricular end systolic volume index; RVOT, right ventricular outflow tract; RVSP, right ventricular systolic pressure; and TOF, tetralogy of Fallot.

content.guideline: The image contains recommendations for routine follow-up, testing intervals, and management strategies for patients with repaired Tetralogy of Fallot (TOF) and residual Pulmonary Regurgitation (PR). The use of cardiac magnetic resonance imaging (CMR) is highlighted as the gold standard for assessing right ventricular size and function. It also describes the importance of delineating coronary artery anatomy in surgical interventions, risk factors for sudden cardiac death (SCD), and the role of cardiac catheterization and symptomatic evaluation in deciding the treatment course.

content.6: There are data indicating that pulmonary valve replacement before specific ventricular size leads to normalization of RV volumes. However, correlation with improved mortality is not evident. Pulmonary valve replacement is recommended if certain criteria are met, including mild to moderate RV or LV systolic dysfunction, severe RV dilation, RV systolic pressure due to RVOT obstruction, and a progressive reduction in exercise tolerance. Pulmonary valve replacement results in the reduction of RV volume and relief of PR.
content.7: Risk factors for SCD include LV systolic or diastolic dysfunction, nonsustained VT, QRS duration ≥180 ms, extensive RV scarring, and inducible sustained VT.
content.8: In patients with repaired TOF and moderate or greater PR undergoing cardiac surgery for another reason, concurrent pulmonary valve replacement may be reasonable.
content.9: Although PR correction is clinically beneficial, valve replacement alone has not consistently shown SCD risk reduction. Including VT surgery and/or ICD implantation is suggested.

Recommendations.Recommendation: Coronary artery compression testing with coronary angiography and balloon dilation is indicated before right ventricle-to-PA conduit stenting or valve placement.
Evaluation for conduit complications in patients with stented right ventricle-to-PA conduits is recommended, including imaging for fractures and thrombus check.
In adults with right ventricle-to-PA conduit and arrhythmia, cardiac catheterization is reasonable to assess hemodynamics.

content.Synopsis: Right ventricle-to-pulmonary artery (PA) conduits are used for treating RVOT obstructive lesions like pulmonary atresia. Conduits may require replacement due to stenosis or valve regurgitation over 10 to 15 years. Coronary artery compression and stent fracture are possible risks.

Therapeutic Recommendations.4: Right ventricle-to-PA conduit intervention is reasonable for adults with moderate or greater conduit stenosis.
Therapeutic Recommendations.5: Right ventricle-to-PA conduit intervention may be reasonable for asymptomatic adults with severe stenosis or regurgitation.

content.Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text: Selective coronary angiography or aortography may be used to determine coronary artery compression. Stent fracture is a common complication.
content.Follow-up and Testing: Outpatient ACHD cardiologist evaluations range from 3 to 36 months depending on physiological stages. ECGs and TTEs are performed routinely, and CMR/CCTs vary in frequency.

clinical_information.Summary: The document outlines recommendations for the management of patients with transposition of the great arteries (d-TGA) with atrial switch, focusing on diagnostic and therapeutic approaches.

Recommendations.Recommendation: Ambulatory monitoring for bradycardia or sinus node dysfunction is recommended for adults with d-TGA with atrial switch, especially if treated with beta blockers or other rate-slowing agents.
Adults with d-TGA with atrial switch repair should undergo annual imaging with either echocardiography or CMR to evaluate for common long-term complications.
Assessment for a communication through the interatrial baffle or venous stenosis is reasonable, particularly if transvenous pacemaker/ICD implantation is considered.
GDMT with attention to anticoagulation is recommended to restore sinus rhythm for adults with d-TGA with atrial switch repair presenting with atrial arrhythmia.

Complex Lesions Discussed: Transposition of the Great Arteries With Atrial Switch

content.Summary: The clinical guideline discusses issues related to patients with d-TGA with atrial switch, including complications like leak across venous baffles, arrhythmias, need for pacemakers/defibrillators, and systolic dysfunction of the systemic ventricle. There is uncertainty about medical therapy for systolic ventricular dysfunction, particularly regarding the use of angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitors, angiotensin-receptor blockers, and aldosterone antagonists. The guideline provides a systematic review and recommendations for the follow-up and testing intervals.
Recommendations: Routine outpatient visits and imaging based on physiological stages
Uncertainty in evidence for using certain medications for systemic right ventricle dysfunction
Concerns: Potential for dysrhythmias due to bradycardia
Limited research supporting primary prevention ICD implantation

content.Title: 2018 ACHD Guideline
Key Points: Imaging for systemic RV function and potential complications should be goal-directed (S4.4.1.1-9).
CMR for quantification of RV function, avoid repetitive gadolinium contrast use (S4.4.1.1-10).
Sustained intra-atrial reentrant tachycardia as a SCD cause (S4.4.1.1-12).
Atrial arrhythmias management includes maintaining sinus rhythm (S4.4.1.1-11, S4.4.1.1-12).
Recommendations for d-TGA with arterial switch (S4.4.1.2-1 to S4.4.1.2-3).

Recommendations.Description: Baseline and serial imaging should be performed in adults with d-TGA with arterial switch and certain conditions (S4.4.1.2-1).
Coronary revascularization should be planned by specialized cardiologists (S4.4.1.2-2).
Perform anatomic evaluation of coronary artery patency in asymptomatic adults (S4.4.1.2-4, S4.4.1.2-5).

Complications after the arterial switch: Stenosis at the arterial anastomotic sites, most commonly supravalvular PS
content.Evaluation for complications: Accomplished by visual imaging such as echocardiography, CCT, and/or CMR.
content.Concerns about coronary abnormalities: In aging asymptomatic patients, stress imaging is not sensitive; coronary abnormalities may progress without notice.

Guidelines regarding coronary interventi.Physiological tests of myocardial perfus: Considered reasonable for adults with d-TGA post arterial switch for assessing symptoms suggestive of myocardial ischemia.
Guidelines regarding coronary interventi.Coronary revascularization: Can be determined based on tests for adults with d-TGA post arterial switch.

Therapeutic recommendations.Aortic valve replacement: Indications for valve replacement should be determined in adults with d-TGA and severe neoarotic valve regurgitation.

Therapeutic recommendations.Pulmonary stenosis (PS) intervention: Recommended in adults with d-TGA and arterial switch with symptoms of HF or decreased exercise capacity.

content.Reoperation considerations for adults af: Include severe RVOT obstruction, neoarotic valve replacement, pulmonary valve replacement, and coronary ostial stenosis treatment.

content.Title: Routine Follow-Up and Testing Intervals with Arterial Switch for Transposition of the Great Arteries (d-TGA)
content.Clinical Focus: Guidelines for routine follow-up and testing in patients with arterial switch for d-TGA, addressing frequency of various tests and considerations for subsequent intervention.
Key Points: Routine MRI may be necessary when clinical indications warrant.
Benchmark assessment (catheter angiography or CT) recommended for adults without coronary artery information.
Once coronary anatomy in an arterial switch recipient is documented, routine imaging is unnecessary without symptoms.
Coronary interventions should be guided by recommendations for atherosclerotic coronary disease.
Valve-sparing root replacement is considered in suitable cases.
content.Surgical Recommendations: Rastelli procedure for patients with d-TGA with VSD and PS: includes an intracardiac baffle and a right ventricle-to-PA conduit.

content.title: Congenitally Corrected Transposition of the Great Arteries

recommendations.description: CMR is reasonable in adults with CCTGA to determine systemic RV dimensions and systolic function.
Tricuspid valve replacement is recommended for symptomatic adults with CCTGA and severe TR, and preserved or mildly depressed systemic ventricular function.
Tricuspid valve replacement is reasonable for asymptomatic adults with CCTGA and severe TR with dilation or mild dysfunction of the systemic ventricle.
Conduit intervention/replacement may be considered for adults with CCTGA and symptomatic subpulmonary left ventricle-to-PA conduit dysfunction.

synopsis: The clinical course of adults with CCTGA often depends on the prevalence and severity of associated cardiac anomalies.
Conduction abnormalities are common, and spontaneous complete heart block prevalence increases with age.
Many adults with CCTGA are referred for tricuspid valve replacement due to dysfunction.
recommendation_specific_supportive_text: CMR is useful for quantification of systemic RV size and function.
Administration of gadolinium contrast is useful in identifying fibrotic myocardium.
Tricuspid valve replacement in adults with dysplastic tricuspid valve.

Recommendations for Fontan Palliation of.Recommendation: New presentation of an atrial tachyarrhythmia in adults with Fontan palliation should be managed promptly.
Adults after Fontan palliation should be evaluated annually with either echocardiography or CMR.
Cardiac catheterization should be performed in adults before initial Fontan surgery.
New onset or worsening atrial tachyarrhythmias in adults with single ventricle after Fontan palliation should prompt a search for potential hemodynamic abnormalities.
In adults with Fontan palliation, it is reasonable to encourage a regular exercise program appropriate to their abilities.
Imaging of the liver and laboratory evaluation of liver function are reasonable in adults after Fontan palliation.

content.general_guideline: This document provides guidelines for managing adults with Fontan palliation. It includes recommendations for biochemical and hematological testing, cardiac catheterization, evaluation for cardiac transplantation, and anticoagulation therapy. Additionally, it addresses catheter ablation, Fontan revision surgery, use of pulmonary vasoactive medications, antiplatelet therapy, and reoperation or intervention for structural/anatomic abnormalities. The synopsis highlights the challenges and possible therapies for managing complications related to the Fontan procedure, such as protein-losing enteropathy and exercise limitation.

content.Fontan surgery is associated with prolon: These arrhythmias might be poorly tolerated, causing serious hemodynamic compromise. Immediate management strategies are critical, preferably coordinated with specialized care for long-term outcomes.

points: Ensure contrast dispersal through the pulmonary vasculature due to streaming of venous return to the PA from multiple separate sources.
Hemodynamic assessment, especially of the pulmonary circulation, is crucial for making informed surgical decisions.
Hemodynamic issues may first present through arrhythmias, necessitating a thorough review of the patient’s function.
Aerobic exercise may aid in maintaining respiratory mechanics and improving exercise capacity in patients with Fontan palliation.
There is increasing recognition of hepatic vulnerability after Fontan palliation, including cirrhosis, impacting surgical or transplantation decisions.
Routine assessment of liver function and structure is important for long-term management.
Routine hemodynamic tests may help identify and address issues early.
Cardiac catheterization should be performed by experts in ACHD coordination.
Venous obstructions with low or no pressure gradients require clinical attention.
Protein-losing enteropathy and plastic bronchitis increase perioperative mortality; transplantation may be needed if medical therapy fails.
Medical therapy for protein-losing enteropathy is often ineffective.
Fontan circulation may predispose to thrombosis; anticoagulation might be required.
Catheter ablation has documented efficacy but may lead to recurrent arrhythmias.
Conversion to total cavopulmonary connection Fontan with Maze may be needed for refractory arrhythmia.

sections.title: Pulmonary Vasocative Medications
sections.details: Medications include endothelin receptor antagonists and PDE-5 inhibitors, aimed at reducing pulmonary vascular resistance and improving cardiac output in Fontan patients. There is potential improvement in pulmonary blood flow and exercise capacity with PDE-5 inhibitors as noted in limited studies.
sections.study_results: A randomized trial of endothelin antagonists showed a modest improvement in peak oxygen consumption and exercise duration. Warfarin use in certain patients demonstrated lower rates of thrombosis.

sections.title: Anticoagulation in Fontan Patients
sections.details: Routine use of anticoagulation with vitamin K antagonists is not strongly recommended based on current evidence. Concerns exist regarding lower function vulnerability when using direct oral anticoagulants.
sections.study_concerns: There are potential increased risks of complications in Fontan patients, cautioning against general recommendation of these agents.

sections.title: Hypoplastic Left Heart Syndrome/Norwood Repair
sections.details: Norwood repair consists of three steps, including anastomosis, and is crucial in early life. The repair addresses related conditions such as occlusive coronary artery disease.
sections.sequelae: Sequelae include anatomical issues contributing to potential coronary ischemia.

sections.title: Truncus Arteriosus
sections.details: Truncus arteriosus, particularly in adults, is often repaired during childhood. Management includes VSD closure and right ventricle-to-PA conduit placement.
sections.physiology: Eisenmenger physiology is common with potential pulmonary hypertension.

sections.title: Double Outlet Right Ventricle
sections.details: Characterized by an aortic and ventricular arrangement that demands specific surgical repairs. Repairs relieve pulmonary stenosis and provide right ventricle-to-PA conduit.

content.Pulmonary Hypertension: Defined as elevation of mean pulmonary arterial pressure to ≥25 mm Hg at rest. It is classified based on the presumed mechanism, including 'postcapillary pulmonary hypertension', parenchymal or restrictive lung disease, among others.
content.SHUNT-Related PAH: In patients with ACHD, PAH can develop perioperatively or years after defect closure. Contributing factors include genetic and environmental exposures.

Recommendations for Severe PAH.Details: Patients with ACHD and pulmonary vascular resistance ≥2.5 Wood units should be assessed collaboratively by an ACHD cardiologist and an expert in pulmonary hypertension.
Adults with septal or great artery shunts should undergo periodic screening for pulmonary hypertension with TTE.
Cardiac catheterization to assess pulmonary vascular hemodynamics is recommended for adults with septal or great artery shunts and signs of pulmonary hypertension.
For adults with septal or great artery shunts, cardiac catheterization with hemodynamics is beneficial to assess suitability for closure.
BNP, chest X-ray, 6-minute walk test, and cardiac catheterization are useful for initial and follow-up evaluation of patients with ACHD with PAH.

content.guideline_title: Pulmonary Hypertension and Eisenmenger Syndrome: Routine Follow-Up and Testing Intervals
recommendation_summary: Patients with ACHD and pulmonary hypertension, especially PAH, have a poorer prognosis compared to those without pulmonary hypertension.
Collaboration between ACHD and pulmonary vascular disease specialists is essential.
Predictors of PAH development in ACHD include anatomical defects, high lung pressures, and certain syndromes like Down syndrome.
Routine follow-up includes assessments through ECG, TTE, Cardiac MRI, and catheterization.
Cardiac catheterization is vital for accurate diagnosis of pulmonary hypertension.
diagnostic_evaluation: Patients should be evaluated for systemic arterial desaturation, venous pressures, and electrocardiographic changes.
Echocardiography can help assess ventricular function and pressures.

Eisenmenger Syndrome.Diagnosis: Clinicians should confirm diagnostic imaging and cardiac catheterization data accuracy and exclude other potential contributors to right-to-left shunting or pulmonary hypertension.
Therapy: Bosentan is beneficial in symptomatic adults with Eisenmenger syndrome with ASD or VSD.
In symptomatic adults with Eisenmenger syndrome, bosentan and PDE-5 inhibitors are reasonable in combination if symptomatic improvement does not occur with either medication alone.
Bosentan is a reasonable therapy for symptomatic adults with complex congenital heart lesions or Down syndrome.
Use of PDE-5 inhibitors is reasonable for symptomatic adults.

content.Synopsis: Eisenmenger syndrome is the most advanced form of PAH associated with congenital intracardiac and great arterial shunting. The syndrome involves elevated pulmonary vascular resistance leading to systemic arterial desaturation. Development is influenced by congenital defects, genetic factors, and environmental exposures.

content.Clinical context: The guideline discusses the management of patients with ACHD and Eisenmenger syndrome. It emphasizes the use of mechanical circulatory support, lung transplantation, and the importance of pharmacological treatments, such as PAH therapies. Recommendations are provided for evaluating and testing patients, as well as the potential benefits and limitations of various treatments.
Recommendations: Right-to-left shunting through septal defects should be accurately diagnosed to guide appropriate therapy.
PH therapies are not recommended if RVOT obstruction is the source of shunting.
Combination PAH therapy shows improved results in certain cases.
Adults with Down syndrome may have different responses to therapies.
Trials: RCTs have shown benefits of a 6-minute walk test after oral bosentan.
Combination PDE-5 inhibitor therapy and endothelin receptor antagonist therapy shows potential benefits.

Factors Related to the Clinical Importan.Age: AAOCA is more commonly linked with SCD in patients <35 years than >35 years, where atherosclerotic coronary disease is more prevalent. However, AAOCA has been attributed to SCD at all ages.
Factors Related to the Clinical Importan.Anatomy of Coronary Ostium and Proximal: Slit-like/fish-mouth-shaped orifice, acute angled takeoff, intramural course, interarterial course and hypoplasia of the proximal coronary artery have been proposed as reasons for symptoms, ischemia, and SCD in patients with AAOCA.
Factors Related to the Clinical Importan.Anomalous Origin: Left coronary artery arising from the right cusp is less common than the right coronary artery arising from the left cusp but is more often found in autopsy series of SCD.
Factors Related to the Clinical Importan.Exercise: Autopsy series suggest most patients die during or in close temporal association with exercise.
Factors Related to the Clinical Importan.Ischemia: Surgical series describe patients with ischemia with MI before surgical repair in the absence of other CAD, suggesting a relation of the coronary anomaly to the ischemia.
Factors Related to the Clinical Importan.Symptoms: In autopsy and surgical series, a significant number of patients reported cardiovascular symptoms, including before SCD events. Symptoms are more commonly reported in patients in whom the left coronary artery arises from the right sinus.

content.Coronary Anomalies: Anomalous aortic origin of a coronary artery, coronary fistula, and myocardial bridge are the most common congenital cardiovascular anomalies.
content.Management Strategies: There are surgical series describing improvements in symptoms after operation. However, data demonstrating surgical repair ameliorates SCD risk are lacking.

content.Title: Anomalous Coronary Artery Evaluation

Recommendations.Recommendation: Coronary angiography, using catheterization, CT, or CMR, is recommended for evaluation of anomalous coronary artery.
Anatomic and physiological evaluation should be performed in patients with anomalous aortic origin of the left coronary from the right sinus and/or right coronary from the left sinus.

content.Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text: CTA, CMR, and catheterization can all delineate the proximal course of the coronary artery and relationship to other structures. CTA is preferred for its superior spatial and temporal resolution. CMR may also provide adequate delineation of the relationship of the coronary artery to the aorta, PA, and other structures.
content.Figure Description: Flowchart on Anomalous Aortic Origin of the Coronary Artery, outlining when surgical intervention is recommended based on ischemic symptoms or ventricular arrhythmias.

Recommendations.Recommendation: Surgery is recommended for AAOCA from the left sinus or AAOCA from the right sinus for symptoms or diagnostic evidence consistent with coronary ischemia attributable to the anomalous coronary artery.
Surgery is reasonable for anomalous aortic origin of the left coronary artery from the right sinus in the absence of symptoms or ischemia.
Surgery for AAOCA is reasonable in the setting of ventricular arrhythmias.

Surgery or continued observation may be reasonable for asymptomatic patients with an anomalous left coronary artery arising from the right sinus or right coronary artery arising from the left sinus without ischemia or anatomic or physiological evaluation suggesting potential for compromise of coronary perfusion.

Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text: In patients with symptoms related to AAOCA, repair of the anomaly should alleviate symptoms.
Anomalous left coronary from the right sinus is less common than anomalous right coronary from the left sinus.

In patients with ventricular arrhythmias presumed related to ischemia caused by anomalous origin of a coronary artery, repair is an option to alleviate the ischemia and presumably mitigate the recurrence of ventricular arrhythmias.
Anomalous aortic origin of the right coronary from the left sinus is more common than anomalous aortic origin of the left coronary from the right sinus.
Anatomic Features: Fish-mouth-shaped or slit-like orifice, intramural course, acute angle.

Recommendations for Anomalous Coronary A.Recommendation: Surgery is recommended for anomalous left coronary artery from the PA.
In a symptomatic adult with anomalous right coronary artery from the PA with symptoms attributed to the anomalous coronary, surgery is recommended.

Surgery for anomalous right coronary artery from the PA is reasonable in an asymptomatic adult with ventricular dysfunction or with myocardial ischemia attributed to anomalous right coronary artery from the PA.

content.Coronary Artery Fistula: Coronary artery fistula is an abnormal communication between a coronary artery and another cardiovascular structure. The incidence in patients undergoing coronary angiography is 0.1% to 0.2%. This can be congenital or acquired. Specific management strategies, such as surgical repair or catheter embolization, have been controversial.
